Observables remain a core part of Angular, especially for HTTP, routing, forms, and asynchronous streams.
How Signals Are Reshaping Angular’s Future
For years, RxJS Observables have been the backbone of Angular applications. Whether it was handling HTTP requests, managing state, reacting to user events, or composing asynchronous operations, Observables became synonymous with Angular itself.
But over the last few Angular releases, a new reactive primitive has entered the picture — Signals.
You’ve probably seen headlines like:
“Observables are dead.”
Not quite.
Observables aren’t disappearing, but Angular’s future is increasingly built around Signals. They simplify state management, reduce boilerplate, improve performance, and unlock features like fine-grained reactivity, hydration, and zoneless change detection.
Let’s explore why Signals matter and what they mean for Angular developers.
The Problem with Traditional Angular Reactivity
Angular’s change detection has always been powerful, but not always efficient.
Imagine a simple counter.
count = 0;
